questions.question: What distinguishes a microcontroller (MCU) from a microprocessor (MPU)?
options: MCUs are always faster than MPUs
MCUs integrate CPU, memory, and peripherals on one chip; MPUs need external components
MPUs are only used in computers
questions.explanation: MCU (e.g., STM32, ESP32): CPU + RAM + Flash + peripherals (ADC, UART, SPI, timers) all on-chip. MPU (e.g., Cortex-A, x86): powerful CPU that requires external RAM, storage, and peripheral chips. MCU = self-contained for embedded tasks.

questions.question: A decoupling capacitor (100nF) placed near an IC's power pins helps reduce high-frequency noise on the power rail.
questions.explanation: Decoupling caps provide local charge storage: when an IC switches and draws sudden current, the cap supplies it instantly (not the distant power supply). 100nF handles MHz noise; 10μF handles slower transients. Place as close to Vcc/GND pins as possible.

questions.question: In PCB design, the technique of surrounding a trace with ground to reduce EMI is called ___.
questions.explanation: Guard traces (grounded traces parallel to sensitive signals) reduce crosstalk and EMI. For critical signals: use guard traces with vias to ground planes every λ/20. Combined with impedance control for RF signals.

questions.question: Your embedded device works on the bench but fails in the field (industrial environment with motors and relays). What's most likely causing failures?

scenario.context: Custom PCB with STM32 MCU. Works perfectly in lab. In factory: random resets, corrupted sensor readings, communication errors.

metrics.Environment: Factory floor near VFDs (variable frequency drives)
metrics.Symptoms: Random MCU resets, SPI errors, ADC noise

options: Software bug that only manifests under certain timing conditions
EMI from motor drives coupling into unshielded traces and inadequate grounding
Temperature — factory is hotter than lab
Component aging — parts degrading after hours of use
questions.explanation: Classic EMI failure pattern: works in quiet lab, fails near motors/VFDs. VFDs generate strong electromagnetic interference at switching frequencies. Fix: proper PCB grounding (solid ground plane), shielded cables, EMI filters on power input, and TVS diodes on signal lines.

questions.question: You need a voltage divider to scale 12V to 3.3V for an ADC input. Using R1 (top) and R2=10kΩ (bottom), what value of R1 is needed?
questions.formula: Vout = Vin × R2/(R1+R2), so R1 = R2 × (Vin/Vout - 1)
questions.explanation: 3.3 = 12 × 10k/(R1+10k). R1+10k = 12×10k/3.3 = 36.36k. R1 = 26.36kΩ. Nearest standard: 27kΩ (gives 3.24V — close enough for ADC with Vref=3.3V). Add a 100nF cap for noise filtering.

questions.question: Match each communication bus to its typical embedded use:
questions.explanation: I²C: 2-wire multi-device (up to 3.4Mbps). SPI: 4-wire full-duplex fast (50+ MHz). CAN: differential bus, fault-tolerant, automotive standard. UART: simplest async serial, good for low-speed links.
